name: Regulatory landscape — EU · Italy · Middle East
description: Supervisory architecture and regulatory landscape across the EU, Italy and the GCC / Islamic-finance world. Quick multiple-choice cards (~1 min each) plus a few open exercises (~30 min). Non-firm-specific market context for risk and quant interviews.

glossary.ECB: European Central Bank — the euro-area central bank; under the SSM it is also the direct prudential supervisor of significant euro-area banks
glossary.SSM: Single Supervisory Mechanism — the EU framework under which the ECB directly supervises significant euro-area banks and oversees national supervisors for others
glossary.EBA: European Banking Authority — EU agency that writes the single rulebook (binding technical standards) and runs the EU-wide stress test; a standard-setter, not a day-to-day supervisor
glossary.CRR: Capital Requirements Regulation — the directly applicable EU regulation implementing Basel quantitative prudential rules (own funds, RWA, leverage, liquidity)
glossary.CRD: Capital Requirements Directive — the EU directive transposed nationally that grants supervisory powers and covers governance, remuneration and Pillar 2
glossary.MREL: Minimum Requirement for Own Funds and Eligible Liabilities — EU bank-specific loss-absorbing and recapitalisation capacity set by the resolution authority under the BRRD
glossary.BRRD: Bank Recovery and Resolution Directive — the EU framework for planning and executing orderly resolution of failing banks, including bail-in and MREL
glossary.SRB: Single Resolution Board — the EU authority that plans and executes resolution of significant banks under the BRRD/SRMR, using bail-in and MREL
glossary.SREP: Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process — the annual ECB supervisory cycle that sets bank-specific Pillar 2 requirements (P2R) and guidance (P2G)
glossary.IFSB: Islamic Financial Services Board — the Kuala Lumpur-based international standard-setter for prudential and supervisory standards for Islamic financial institutions
glossary.AAOIFI: Accounting and Auditing Organization for Islamic Financial Institutions — the Bahrain-based body setting Sharia, accounting and governance standards for Islamic finance
glossary.PSIA: Profit-Sharing Investment Account — an Islamic-bank funding account, typically structured as a mudarabah, where the holder supplies capital and shares profit and loss
glossary.ESMA: European Securities and Markets Authority — the EU-level regulator for securities markets and investor protection, overseeing MiFID II/MiFIR and related conduct rules
glossary.EIOPA: European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority — the EU body overseeing insurers and pension funds under Solvency II

primer:
**EU architecture.** Under the **Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM)** the **ECB** directly supervises *significant* euro-area banks (e.g. Intesa Sanpaolo, UniCredit); national competent authorities supervise *less-significant* ones under ECB oversight (via joint supervisory teams). The **EBA** writes the *single rulebook* (binding technical standards) and runs the EU-wide *stress test* — a standard-setter, **not** a day-to-day supervisor. Other EU bodies: **ESMA** (securities markets, MiFID II/MiFIR, investor protection), **EIOPA** (insurance & pensions under Solvency II), **SRB** (Single Resolution Mechanism — resolution via bail-in and **MREL**, under BRRD/SRMR). Basel enters EU law through the **CRR** (directly applicable) and **CRD** (transposed nationally); **CRR3/CRD6** bring the Basel III finalisation, including the **output floor** (internal-model RWA $\\ge$ **72.5%** of standardised, phasing in from 1 Jan 2025). The **leverage ratio** minimum is **3%** of Tier 1 to total exposure. The **SREP** sets, per bank, a binding *Pillar 2 requirement (P2R)* plus non-binding *Pillar 2 guidance (P2G)* — and the EBA stress test is an *input* to it, not a pass/fail; a weak result raises P2G, with graded consequences (capital plan, payout limits, ultimately SRB resolution). The harmonised **Deposit Guarantee** covers **€100,000** per depositor per bank. **IFRS 9** provisions via a three-stage *expected-credit-loss* model (Stage 1 = 12-month ECL; Stages 2–3 = lifetime). **AnaCredit** is the ECB's granular, loan-level credit dataset. **Expected Shortfall** $\\ge$ VaR at the same confidence (it is the mean loss beyond VaR).

**Italy.** **Banca d'Italia** is the central bank and national supervisor (leads for less-significant banks under the SSM, runs macroprudential policy); its **Centrale dei Rischi** is the national central credit register. **CONSOB** = securities markets and investor protection (Italy's ESMA counterpart); **IVASS** = insurers (Solvency II); **COVIP** = supplementary private pension funds. Banking law = **Testo Unico Bancario (TUB)**; financial-markets law = **TUF**. **GACS** = a state guarantee on *senior tranches of NPL securitisations*, used to offload the large post-2011 **non-performing-loan** stock. The 2015–16 reforms grouped small mutual banks (**BCC**) into cooperative banking groups (Iccrea, Cassa Centrale). The *anti-usury* regime publishes threshold rates (*tassi soglia*) above which a loan is usurious.

**Gulf / Islamic finance.** Onshore regulators: **SAMA** (Saudi Arabia), **CBUAE** (UAE), **QCB** (Qatar onshore), **CBB** (Bahrain, a single integrated regulator). Common-law *free zones* have their own regulators: **DFSA** (DIFC, Dubai), **FSRA** (ADGM, Abu Dhabi), **QFCRA** (QFC, Doha). GCC supervisors broadly implement **Basel III**, often with high capital ratios. Standard-setters: the **IFSB** (Kuala Lumpur) issues *prudential* standards for Islamic financial institutions (adapting Basel); **AAOIFI** (Bahrain) issues *Sharia, accounting and governance* standards. Islamic finance prohibits **riba** (interest/usury), **gharar** (excessive uncertainty) and **maysir** (gambling), and requires real *asset-backing* and risk-sharing (so guaranteed fixed returns are not allowed). A **sukuk** is a Sharia-compliant *certificate of ownership* in an underlying asset/venture — returns come from real assets, not interest, so holders bear asset risk (unlike a conventional bond). A *profit-sharing investment account (PSIA)* is structured as a **mudarabah**: the holder supplies capital, the bank manages it, and both share profit/loss (the holder bears investment losses absent misconduct). Under IFSB capital rules, assets funded by unrestricted PSIA are largely *removed* from risk-weighted assets, but supervisors keep an **'alpha'** fraction to reflect *displaced commercial risk* (banks smoothing payouts to retain depositors). EU market entry: a Gulf bank generally needs an authorised EU *subsidiary* (full CRR/CRD, ECB if significant) rather than a *branch* (narrower, no EU passport), since third-country *equivalence* is patchy.

cards.term: [EU] Which authority directly supervises 'significant' euro-area banks?
cards.sub: A) the ECB, through the SSM    B) the European Commission, through its financial-stability directorate    C) the [[EBA]], through its college-of-supervisors mandate    D) each national central bank acting on its own authority
cards.definition: Correct: A) the ECB, through the [[SSM]]. Under the Single Supervisory Mechanism the [[ECB]] directly supervises significant institutions; national competent authorities supervise less significant ones under [[ECB]] oversight. The [[EBA]] writes rules but does not supervise individual banks.

cards.term: [EU] The EBA's main role is to:
cards.sub: A) write the single rulebook and run EU-wide stress tests    B) plan and execute the resolution of failing cross-border banks    C) set the euro-area monetary-policy stance and interest rates    D) carry out the day-to-day supervision of the largest banks
cards.definition: Correct: A) write the single rulebook and run EU-wide stress tests. The European Banking Authority issues binding technical standards (the single rulebook) and coordinates the EU-wide stress test; it is a standard-setter, not a day-to-day supervisor. (Distinct from the [[ECB]], which directly supervises banks under the [[SSM]].)

cards.term: [EU] Basel standards enter EU law mainly through:
cards.sub: A) Solvency II    B) the BRRD    C) the CRR and CRD    D) MiFID II
cards.definition: Correct: C) the [[CRR]] and [[CRD]]. The Capital Requirements Regulation (directly applicable across member states) and the Capital Requirements Directive (transposed nationally) implement Basel for banks. CRR3/CRD6 bring in the Basel III finalisation; enforcement sits with the [[ECB]]/[[SSM]].

cards.term: [EU] The Basel III 'output floor' introduced by CRR3:
cards.sub: A) caps a bank's total balance-sheet assets relative to capital    B) fixes the level of the harmonised EU deposit guarantee    C) sets the binding minimum leverage ratio at 3% of exposure    D) limits internal-model RWA to 72.5% of the standardised figure
cards.definition: Correct: D) limits internal-model RWA to 72.5% of the standardised figure. The output floor stops internal models producing risk-weighted assets below 72.5% of the standardised approach, capping model-driven capital savings. It phases in from 1 January 2025 under [[CRR]]3/[[CRD]]6.

cards.term: [EU] Insurance and pensions across the EU are overseen by:
cards.sub: A) ESMA    B) the SRB    C) the EBA    D) EIOPA
cards.definition: Correct: D) [[EIOPA]]. [[EIOPA]] oversees insurers and pension funds under the Solvency II framework. [[ESMA]] covers securities markets, the [[EBA]] banks, and the [[SRB]] bank resolution.

cards.term: [EU] MiFID II / MiFIR (securities markets, investor protection) sit with:
cards.sub: A) EIOPA    B) the ECB    C) the EBA    D) ESMA
cards.definition: Correct: D) [[ESMA]]. The European Securities and Markets Authority is the EU-level body for securities markets and investor protection under MiFID II/MiFIR. [[EBA]] covers banks; [[EIOPA]] covers insurance.

cards.term: [EU] The ECB's SREP produces, for each bank, a:
cards.sub: A) fixed remuneration rate on overnight central-bank deposits    B) mandatory ban on any payment of ordinary dividends    C) Pillar 2 requirement (P2R) and guidance (P2G)    D) hard cap on the bank's permitted leverage exposure
cards.definition: Correct: C) Pillar 2 requirement (P2R) and guidance (P2G). The [[SREP]] sets a binding Pillar 2 requirement plus non-binding Pillar 2 guidance on top of the Pillar 1 minimum, using the [[EBA]] stress-test result as an input.

cards.term: [EU] The body running the Single Resolution Mechanism is the:
cards.sub: A) Single Resolution Board (SRB)    B) European Commission    C) [[ESMA]]    D) [[ECB]]
cards.definition: Correct: A) Single Resolution Board ([[SRB]]). The [[SRB]] plans and executes resolution of significant banks using tools such as bail-in and the [[MREL]] requirement, under the [[BRRD]]/SRMR.

cards.term: [EU] The harmonised EU deposit guarantee covers, per depositor per bank:
cards.sub: A) €50,000    B) an unlimited amount    C) €250,000    D) €100,000
cards.definition: Correct: D) €100,000. The Deposit Guarantee Schemes Directive sets coverage at €100,000 per depositor per institution.

cards.term: [EU] IFRS 9 measures loan loss provisions using:
cards.sub: A) a three-stage expected-credit-loss model    B) mark-to-market fair value through profit or loss only    C) a single incurred-loss model triggered at default    D) only the Basel regulatory expected-loss figure
cards.definition: Correct: A) a three-stage expected-credit-loss model. IFRS 9 stages exposures 1–3: Stage 1 carries 12-month expected credit loss; Stages 2–3 carry lifetime ECL once credit risk has risen significantly or the asset is impaired.

cards.term: [EU] AnaCredit is:
cards.sub: A) an adverse macro-financial stress-test scenario    B) a pan-European credit rating agency for sovereigns    C) the ECB's granular loan-level credit dataset    D) an EU-wide 'bad bank' for non-performing loans
cards.definition: Correct: C) the ECB's granular loan-level credit dataset. AnaCredit (Analytical Credit Datasets) collects harmonised, loan-level data on credit to legal entities across the euro area.

cards.term: [EU] The Basel/CRR leverage-ratio minimum is:
cards.definition: Correct: A) 3%. The leverage ratio requires Tier 1 capital of at least 3% of total exposure — a simple, non-risk-based backstop to risk-weighted requirements.

cards.term: [Italy] Italy's central bank and national banking supervisor is:
cards.sub: A) IVASS    B) Banca d'Italia    C) CONSOB    D) the MEF
cards.definition: Correct: B) Banca d'Italia. Banca d'Italia is the central bank and national competent authority; it directly supervises less-significant Italian banks under the [[SSM]] and runs macroprudential policy.

cards.term: [Italy] Italy's largest banks (e.g. Intesa Sanpaolo, UniCredit) are directly supervised by:
cards.sub: A) CONSOB, as listed-securities issuers    B) the [[EBA]], as systemic EU institutions    C) the ECB, as significant institutions    D) Banca d'Italia acting entirely on its own
cards.definition: Correct: C) the ECB, as significant institutions. As significant institutions they fall under direct ECB supervision; Banca d'Italia participates through the joint supervisory teams.

cards.term: [Italy] The Italian insurance supervisor is:
cards.sub: A) CONSOB    B) Banca d'Italia    C) IVASS    D) COVIP
cards.definition: Correct: C) IVASS. IVASS (Istituto per la Vigilanza sulle Assicurazioni) supervises insurers under Solvency II and works closely with Banca d'Italia.

cards.term: [Italy] CONSOB is responsible for:
cards.sub: A) supplementary private pension funds    B) the conduct of national monetary policy    C) bank capital and prudential requirements    D) securities markets and investor protection
cards.definition: Correct: D) securities markets and investor protection. CONSOB (Commissione Nazionale per le Società e la Borsa) supervises securities markets, prospectuses and market conduct — Italy's counterpart to ESMA.

cards.term: [Italy] Supplementary (private) pension funds in Italy are supervised by:
cards.sub: A) IVASS    B) CONSOB    C) INPS    D) COVIP
cards.definition: Correct: D) COVIP. COVIP (Commissione di Vigilanza sui Fondi Pensione) supervises Italy's funded supplementary pension schemes.

cards.term: [Italy] The consolidated law governing Italian banking is the:
cards.sub: A) Testo Unico Bancario (TUB)    B) Testo Unico della Finanza (TUF)    C) MiFID    D) Codice Civile
cards.definition: Correct: A) Testo Unico Bancario (TUB). The Testo Unico Bancario (Legislative Decree 385/1993) governs banking; the TUF (D.Lgs 58/1998) governs financial markets and intermediaries.

cards.term: [Italy] 'GACS' refers to:
cards.sub: A) an adverse supervisory stress-test scenario    B) an AAOIFI Sharia-compliance standard for sukuk    C) a state guarantee on senior tranches of NPL securitisations    D) a national deposit-guarantee scheme for savers
cards.definition: Correct: C) a state guarantee on senior tranches of NPL securitisations. The Garanzia Cartolarizzazione Sofferenze is an Italian state guarantee on the senior notes of bad-loan securitisations, used to help banks offload non-performing loans.

cards.term: [Italy] Banca d'Italia's 'Centrale dei Rischi' is:
cards.sub: A) the national central credit register    B) the resolution fund for failing Italian banks    C) the national retail-payments clearing system    D) the domestic sovereign credit rating agency
cards.definition: Correct: A) the national central credit register. The Centrale dei Rischi records borrowers' exposures across lenders — a core data source for credit-risk assessment in Italy.

cards.term: [Italy] A defining feature of the Italian banking system in the 2010s was:
cards.sub: A) the complete absence of any cooperative banks    B) majority ownership of major banks by the [[ECB]]    C) negligible levels of non-performing loans    D) a large stock of non-performing loans needing work-out
cards.definition: Correct: D) a large stock of non-performing loans needing work-out. The post-2011 recession left Italian banks with one of Europe's largest NPL stocks, driving disposals, GACS and [[ECB]] NPL guidance.

cards.term: [Italy] The 2015–16 reforms reorganised Italy's small mutual banks (BCC) into:
cards.sub: A) insurance firms    B) foreign branches    C) cooperative banking groups (e.g. Iccrea, Cassa Centrale)    D) a single state bank
cards.definition: Correct: C) cooperative banking groups (e.g. Iccrea, Cassa Centrale). The banche di credito cooperativo were consolidated under cohesive groups to strengthen capital and enable consolidated supervision.

cards.term: [Italy] Italy's anti-usury regime works by periodically publishing:
cards.sub: A) threshold rates (tassi soglia) above which a loan is usurious    B) the Basel III output floor for internal models    C) the central bank's main refinancing policy rate    D) the statutory ceiling of the deposit guarantee
cards.definition: Correct: A) threshold rates (tassi soglia) above which a loan is usurious. Average market rates are surveyed and a statutory multiple sets the usury threshold (tasso soglia) that lenders must not exceed.

cards.term: [Gulf] SAMA is the central bank and regulator of:
cards.sub: A) Saudi Arabia    B) Qatar    C) Bahrain    D) the United Arab Emirates
cards.definition: Correct: A) Saudi Arabia. The Saudi Central Bank (still abbreviated SAMA) regulates banks, insurers and finance companies in Saudi Arabia.

cards.term: [Gulf] Onshore banks in the UAE are supervised by:
cards.sub: A) the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE)    B) the ADGM FSRA    C) SAMA    D) the DFSA
cards.definition: Correct: A) the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE). The CBUAE supervises onshore UAE banks; the DFSA and ADGM's FSRA regulate the DIFC and ADGM financial free zones respectively.
cards.note: [1] Central Bank of the UAE — https://www.centralbank.ae/

cards.term: [Gulf] The DFSA regulates:
cards.sub: A) the DIFC free zone in Dubai    B) the Qatar Financial Centre (QFC) in Doha    C) all banks across the United Arab Emirates    D) onshore Saudi banks and finance companies
cards.definition: Correct: A) the DIFC free zone in Dubai. The Dubai Financial Services Authority is the independent regulator of the Dubai International Financial Centre, a common-law financial free zone.

cards.term: [Gulf] Firms in Abu Dhabi Global Market are regulated by the:
cards.sub: A) DFSA    B) QFCRA    C) CBUAE    D) FSRA
cards.definition: Correct: D) FSRA. The Financial Services Regulatory Authority regulates the ADGM, Abu Dhabi's common-law financial free zone.

cards.term: [Gulf] Bahrain's single integrated financial regulator is the:
cards.sub: A) QCB    B) CBB    C) DFSA    D) CMA
cards.definition: Correct: B) CBB. The Central Bank of Bahrain regulates banking, insurance and capital markets; Bahrain is a long-standing Islamic-finance hub.

cards.term: [Gulf] Qatar's onshore regulator is the QCB; the Qatar Financial Centre is regulated by the:
cards.sub: A) QFCRA    B) FSRA    C) SAMA    D) DFSA
cards.definition: Correct: A) QFCRA. The Qatar Financial Centre Regulatory Authority regulates firms in the QFC, separate from the Qatar Central Bank's onshore remit.

cards.term: [Gulf] On Basel adoption, GCC regulators have broadly:
cards.sub: A) rejected the Basel framework outright    B) adopted only the original Basel I accord    C) implemented Basel III, often with high capital ratios    D) imposed no binding capital requirements at all
cards.definition: Correct: C) implemented Basel III, often with high capital ratios. GCC supervisors implement Basel III, and regional banks typically hold capital comfortably above the minimum ratios.
cards.note: [1] Central Bank of the UAE — https://www.centralbank.ae/

cards.term: [Gulf] The IFSB sets:
cards.sub: A) regional crude-oil benchmark prices    B) prudential standards for Islamic financial institutions    C) individual Sharia rulings for retail customers    D) the EU's harmonised bank capital rules
cards.definition: Correct: B) prudential standards for Islamic financial institutions. The [[IFSB]] (Kuala Lumpur) issues prudential and supervisory standards — capital adequacy, risk management — adapting Basel to Islamic finance. [[AAOIFI]] covers Sharia and accounting standards.

cards.term: [Gulf] AAOIFI, based in Bahrain, issues:
cards.sub: A) regional monetary-policy decisions    B) credit ratings for sovereign sukuk issuers    C) Sharia, accounting and governance standards for Islamic finance    D) approvals for sukuk stock-exchange listings
cards.definition: Correct: C) Sharia, accounting and governance standards for Islamic finance. The [[AAOIFI]] sets widely adopted Sharia and accounting standards; the [[IFSB]] handles the complementary prudential (capital/risk) standards.

cards.term: [Gulf] 'Riba', which Islamic finance prohibits, refers to:
cards.sub: A) interest / usury    B) short-selling of securities    C) excessive contractual uncertainty    D) speculative gambling-like contracts
cards.definition: Correct: A) interest / usury. Islamic finance bars riba (interest), and also gharar (excessive uncertainty) and maysir (gambling), favouring asset-backed, risk-sharing structures.

cards.term: [Gulf] A sukuk is best described as:
cards.sub: A) a Sharia-compliant certificate of ownership in an underlying asset or venture    B) an ordinary listed equity share carrying voting rights    C) a conventional fixed-coupon interest-bearing bond    D) a profit-sharing bank deposit account for savers
cards.definition: Correct: A) a Sharia-compliant certificate of ownership in an underlying asset or venture. Sukuk pay returns generated by real assets or projects rather than interest, so holders bear ownership/asset risk. This structure is governed by [[AAOIFI]] Sharia standards and [[IFSB]] prudential rules.

cards.term: [Gulf] A profit-sharing investment account (PSIA) is typically structured as a:
cards.sub: A) mudarabah where the bank invests the funds and shares profit/loss    B) fixed-rate term deposit with a guaranteed return    C) block of ordinary equity in the bank itself    D) senior unsecured loan extended to the bank
cards.definition: Correct: A) mudarabah where the bank invests the funds and shares profit/loss. Under a mudarabah the holder supplies capital and the bank manages it; returns depend on actual profit and the holder bears investment losses (absent misconduct). The [[IFSB]] governs how [[PSIA]]-funded assets are treated in capital calculations.

cards.term: [Gulf] Under IFSB capital rules, assets funded by unrestricted PSIA are:
cards.sub: A) fully included, at par, in the capital-ratio denominator    B) partly excluded, with an 'alpha' share kept for displaced commercial risk    C) never made subject to any capital requirement at all    D) charged double the standard regulatory capital
cards.definition: Correct: B) partly excluded, with an 'alpha' share kept for displaced commercial risk. Because [[PSIA]] holders absorb investment risk, their funded assets are largely removed from risk-weighted assets — but supervisors retain an 'alpha' fraction to reflect displaced commercial risk (the bank smoothing returns to retain depositors). The [[IFSB]] capital standards define the alpha parameter.

cards.term: [Italy] You must validate the IRB credit-risk model of a mid-sized Italian bank that still carries a legacy non-performing-loan (NPL) book. Outline your approach and what is specific to the Italian/EU context.
cards.sub: Open-ended exercise — structure a full answer in ~30 min (written or spoken).
cards.definition: This tests whether you can run a structured validation and then layer the local context on top, not whether you can list Italian acronyms. Start generic: is the model still fit for use? Check that the current portfolio still resembles the development data, that it still separates good borrowers from bad, that predicted default rates match what actually happened, and that it is stable and properly used. Then add what is Italy/EU-specific and reason about how each one bites. A legacy NPL book means the historical data is dominated by work-out and disposal dynamics — and state-guaranteed GACS securitisations may have removed bad loans from the book — so the default sample may not represent fresh lending. The EU harmonised definition of default and supervisory 'calendar provisioning' change how and when defaults and losses are recognised, which can break comparability with older vintages. Supervision is split: Banca d'Italia leads for a less-significant bank, the [[ECB]] via joint supervisory teams (under the [[SSM]]) if it is significant, so expectations and documentation must match the relevant supervisor. The [[EBA]] sets the binding technical standards on IRB model requirements. Conclude with a clear call: recalibrate if the ranking still holds but levels have drifted; redevelop if discrimination has decayed or the book has structurally changed. The aptitude on show is adapting a general method to a specific market.
cards.note: [1] Banca d'Italia — https://www.bancaditalia.it/ · [2] ECB internal models — https://www.bankingsupervision.europa.eu/

cards.term: [Gulf] Design the capital-adequacy treatment for an Islamic bank funded largely by profit-sharing investment accounts (PSIA). How does it differ from a conventional bank?
cards.sub: Open-ended exercise — structure a full answer in ~30 min (written or spoken).
cards.definition: They want you to reason from how the funding actually behaves, not to recite [[IFSB]] clauses. The key insight: [[PSIA]] holders are not depositors owed a fixed return — under a mudarabah they share in profits and bear investment losses, so economically they absorb risk more like investors than creditors. Reason through the consequence for capital: if those holders genuinely take the asset risk, the assets they fund should not all sit in the bank's risk-weighted denominator the way a conventional loan book does, so a large share is excluded and required capital falls. Then introduce the realistic complication: in practice banks smooth payouts to stop depositors leaving (displaced commercial risk), so the bank effectively bears some of that risk after all — which is why supervisors keep an 'alpha' fraction of [[PSIA]]-funded assets in the calculation rather than excluding them entirely. Round it out with constraints a conventional model ignores: no interest (riba) and no excessive uncertainty (gharar); asset-backed structures (sukuk, murabaha, ijara); and a Sharia board signing off products per [[AAOIFI]] standards. The aptitude is reasoning from who bears the risk to how much capital is needed, and noticing that theory (full risk transfer) and practice (displaced commercial risk) diverge.
cards.note: [1] Islamic Financial Services Board — https://www.ifsb.org/ · [2] AAOIFI — https://aaoifi.com/

cards.term: [EU] A significant euro-area bank comes out badly in the EBA adverse stress-test scenario. Walk through what actually happens next.
cards.sub: Open-ended exercise — structure a full answer in ~30 min (written or spoken).
cards.definition: This checks that you understand the supervisory feedback loop, not just the modelling. The EU stress test is not a hard pass/fail — it is an input. Trace the chain: the projected capital depletion under the adverse scenario feeds the [[ECB]]'s annual [[SREP]], which turns a weak result into higher Pillar 2 guidance — the buffer the bank is expected to hold above its binding requirements for exactly this kind of stress. Sitting in or near that buffer triggers supervisory dialogue and constraints rather than instant failure: a credible capital plan, possible limits on dividends and buy-backs, and closer monitoring. Reason about the bank's levers — raise capital, cut risk-weighted assets, retain earnings — and the trade-offs of each. Then note the escalation path if capital actually falls toward the combined buffer or the minimum: automatic distribution restrictions, and ultimately the resolution machinery ([[SRB]], bail-in, [[MREL]]) if the bank becomes non-viable. The [[EBA]] stress test is run under rules set by the [[CRR]]/[[CRD]]. The aptitude is showing you grasp stress testing as a governance mechanism with graded consequences.
cards.note: [1] EBA stress testing — https://www.eba.europa.eu/ · [2] ECB Banking Supervision — https://www.bankingsupervision.europa.eu/

cards.term: [Gulf] A Gulf bank wants to expand into the EU. Compare how it would be regulated there versus at home, and the choices it faces.
cards.sub: Open-ended exercise — structure a full answer in ~30 min (written or spoken).
cards.definition: They are probing whether you can reason across two regulatory regimes, not recall statutes. Frame the core choice: to do meaningful banking business in the EU the bank generally needs an authorised EU presence — typically a subsidiary, an EU-incorporated bank supervised under the same [[CRR]]/[[CRD]] rules as any other (and, if significant, directly by the [[ECB]] under the [[SSM]]), versus a branch, which has narrower scope and no cross-border EU passport. Contrast with home: GCC supervision (say CBUAE or SAMA) is Basel III-based too, so the prudential language rhymes, but the EU layers on the single rulebook, harmonised resolution ([[SRB]]/[[MREL]]), an EU deposit guarantee, and conduct regimes (MiFID II / [[ESMA]]) that can be heavier than at home. Reason about the practical decision factors: which activities (retail, wholesale, markets), how much capital and liquidity gets trapped locally, whether to lean on a free-zone track record (DIFC/ADGM common-law) as a bridge, and Islamic-finance products needing EU-compatible structuring per [[IFSB]] standards. Close on third-country 'equivalence' being patchy, so a subsidiary is usually the robust route. The aptitude is mapping one framework onto another and surfacing the trade-offs.
cards.note: [1] ECB Banking Supervision — https://www.bankingsupervision.europa.eu/ · [2] Central Bank of the UAE — https://www.centralbank.ae/

cards.term: [EU] The EBA directly supervises significant euro-area banks.
cards.definition: False — the [[ECB]] directly supervises significant institutions through the [[SSM]]. The [[EBA]] writes the single rulebook and runs the EU-wide stress test under the [[CRR]]/[[CRD]] framework, but it does not supervise individual banks.

cards.term: [EU] Expected Shortfall is always at least as large as VaR at the same confidence level.
cards.summary: True — ES is the average loss beyond VaR, so it can't be smaller.
cards.definition: True. Expected Shortfall is the mean loss in the tail beyond the VaR threshold, so by construction ES ≥ VaR at the same confidence — which is why it captures tail severity that VaR ignores.

cards.term: [Gulf] Sukuk pay a fixed rate of interest, just like a conventional bond.
cards.summary: False — returns come from real assets, not interest (riba is prohibited).
cards.definition: False. Sukuk are Sharia-compliant certificates of ownership in an underlying asset or venture; their returns are generated by those real assets, not by interest (riba), which is prohibited. Holders therefore bear asset/ownership risk. [[AAOIFI]] and [[IFSB]] standards govern their structure and prudential treatment.

cards.term: [EU] Which of these are EU-level supervisory or standard-setting authorities? Select all that apply.
cards.summary: EBA, ECB and EIOPA are EU bodies; SAMA is Saudi Arabia's central bank.
cards.definition: The [[EBA]] (banks/rulebook), the [[ECB]] ([[SSM]] supervision) and [[EIOPA]] (insurance & pensions) are EU authorities. SAMA is the Saudi Central Bank, not an EU body.

cards.term: [Gulf] Which principles must Sharia-compliant finance respect? Select all that apply.
cards.summary: Prohibits riba and gharar and requires asset-backing; guaranteed fixed returns are not allowed.
cards.definition: Islamic finance prohibits riba (interest) and gharar (excessive uncertainty) and requires real asset-backing and risk-sharing. Guaranteed fixed returns are not permitted, since the provider of capital must share in risk. These principles underpin [[IFSB]] and [[AAOIFI]] standards.

cards.term: [EU] The Basel leverage-ratio minimum is {{3}}% of Tier 1 capital to total exposure.
cards.summary: 3% — a non-risk-based backstop to the risk-weighted requirements.
cards.definition: The leverage ratio requires Tier 1 capital of at least 3% of total exposure, a simple backstop that does not depend on risk weights.

cards.term: [Gulf] An Islamic profit-sharing investment account is structured as a {{mudarabah}}, where the bank invests the funds and shares profit and loss with the holder.
cards.summary: A mudarabah — the holder supplies capital, the bank manages it, and both share the outcome.
cards.definition: Under a mudarabah the account holder provides capital and the bank acts as manager; returns depend on the actual profit earned, and the holder bears investment losses absent misconduct. This risk-sharing is why PSIA-funded assets get special capital treatment under IFSB rules.
